tools — Micellaneous language tools¶

New in version 0.1.

Module author: Claude Gibert <claude.gibert@synopticview.co.uk>

Miscellaneous classes and functions¶

The tools module offers different convenience classes and functions. It is probably quicker to look at the source file to find out what things do.

core.tools.specialise(newclass)¶
Parameters:newclass (any class) – The class to change to

Dynamically changes the current class of the object to newclass.

core.tools.classname()¶

returns the class name of the current object.

core.tools.classof()¶

returns the class object of the current object.

core.tools.is_string(what)¶

returns True or False

core.tools.to_list(item)¶
Parameters:item (any type) – any variable

create a list out of a single element or returns it untouched if it is already a list ot a tuple.

core.tools.no_list(item)¶
Parameters:item (any type) – any variable

returns the first element if it is a one element list or tuple. Raises ValueError if the list has more than one element.

core.tools.is_list(item)¶
Parameters:item (any type) – any variable

returns True or False

core.tools.is_tuple(item)¶
Parameters:item (any type) – any variable

returns True or False

core.tools.is_tuple_or_list(item)¶
Parameters:item (any type) – any variable

returns True or False

core.tools.is_dict(item)¶
Parameters:item (any type) – any variable

returns True or False

core.tools.to_lower(item)¶
Parameters:item (any type) – any variable

recursively converts any arbitrary stucture to lower case. It is aware of strings tuples, lists and dictionary and converts any string contained to lower case. For example:

a = [['Hello','World'],{'a':'HELLO','b':'world'},'SHOUT']
print to_lower(a)
[['hello', 'world'], {'a': 'hello', 'b': 'world'}, 'shout']
core.tools.float10Power(value)¶

returns the power of 10 of a float, postive or negative value

core.tools.floatFormat(power)¶

returns a Python type format using the power of 10 given as a parameter. Numbers less than 10^-2 or more than 10^+2 are formatted using the exponant notation.

core.tools.apply_type(thisType, item)¶
Parameters:
  • thisType (any) – a valid python class
  • item (any) – any variable

builds an instance of ‘thisType’ initialised with item, or if item is a list, builds a list of instances of ‘thisType’, for example:

a = ['12','24','45','50']
print apply_type(int,a)
[12, 24, 45, 50]
core.tools.apply_type_deep(thisType, item)¶
Parameters:
  • thisType (any) – a valid python class
  • item (any) – any variable

recursively builds instances of ‘thisType’ in arbitrary stucture with item going through lists and dictionaries (hence deep), for example:

a = ['12','24','45',{'a':'12','b':13},'50']
print apply_type_deep(int,a)
[12, 24, 45, {'a': 12, 'b': 13}, 50]
core.tools.resolve_environment_variables(what)¶
Parameters:what (string) – contains text and possibly environment variable name in the form: ${VARIABLE}

This function looks in the shell environment and substitutes each variable name with its value:

s = "Map over ${DOMAIN}"
core.tools.substitute_variables(template, what)¶
Parameters:
  • template (string) – containing the templated text
  • what (dictionary) – containing entries with the same name as the variables

template is a string containing variable following the format: “<var1> <var2> etc...”.

template = "<type> for <valid>, parameter <parameter>, level <level>"
what = dict(
    type = 'forecast',
    valid = 2010121500,
    parameter = 't',
    level = 500
)
print substitute_variables(template,what)
forecast for 2010121500, parameter t, level 500

This is a convenience function, it first calls the function find_variables and then the function substitute_variable_list.

core.tools.find_variables(template)¶
Parameters:template (string) – containing the templated text

Returns the list of variable names contained in the string template please see substitute_variables() above.

core.tools.substitute_variable_list(variables, template, what)¶
Parameters:
  • variables (list of strings) – the list of variables to be substituted in the template, typically returned by the function find_variables
  • template (string) – containing the templated text
  • what (dictionary) – containing entries with the same name as the variables

Please see substitute_variables() above.

core.tools.substitute_unix_dates(template, name1, thedate1, ..., nameN, thedateN)¶
Parameters:
  • template (string) – containing the templated text
  • name (string) – used in the template for the date following, these work in pairs
  • thedate (core.date.Date) – to be formatatted and use for the substitution

template is a string containing dates expressed as: name(strftime-format) where: name is here the string preceding the (format), e.g. date(%d). The format complies with the Unix format for the function strftime:

template = "forecast/Ydate(%Y)/Mdate(%m)/Ddate(%d)/Hdate(%H)/date(%Y%m%d_%Hz)+valid(%Y%m%d_%H%Mz).hdf
a = substitute_unix_dates(template,'date',Date(2010010100))
print substitute_unix_dates(template,'valid',Date(2010010212))
forecast/Y2010/M10/D01/H00/20100101_00z+20100102_1200z

Please note that for convenience in configuration files (see core.configfile.ConfigFile), the format can also be expressed using the character ‘@’ instead of ‘%’.

core.tools.substitute_unix_calculated_dates(template, date, keyword = 'date')¶
Parameters:
  • template (string) – containing the templated text
  • date (core.date.Date) – a date
  • keyword (string) – the name of the variable in the template, see below

This function is similar to substitute_unix_dates(), it substitutes dates formatted as strftime-format, but dates can include simple calculations:

s = 'date(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz)-date+12(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz)-date-12(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz)'
date = Date(2011020100)
print substitute_unix_calculated_dates(s,date)
  -> 20110201_0000z-20110201_1200z-20110131_1200z

The calculations depend on the unit of the date:

s = 'date(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz)-date+12(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz)-date-12(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz)'
date = Date(20110201)
print substitute_unix_calculated_dates(s,date)
  -> 20110201_0000z-20110213_0000z-20110120_0000z

Finally, you can combine the use of this function with subtitute_variables() to express the calculation in terms of variables part of a dictionary (please also note the use of the keyword parameter):

d = dict(step=12)
s = 'time(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz)-time+<step>(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz)-time-<step>(@Y@m@d_@H@Mz)'
s = substitute_variables(s,d)
date = Date(2011020100)
print substitute_unix_calculated_dates(s,date,keyword='time')
  -> 20110201_0000z-20110213_0000z-20110120_0000z

Please note that for convenience in configuration files (see core.configfile.ConfigFile), the format can also be expressed using the character ‘@’ instead of ‘%’.

class core.tools.dictionary(object)¶

inherits from the built-in dict and redefines the __str__ method to improve the display of the dictionary, particularly with nested dictionaries.

class core.tools.nocasedict(dictionary)¶

inherits from dictionary but also convert the dictionary keys to lower case when reading and writing.

core.tools.mergedicts_overwrite(a, b)¶

return a new dictionary after merging dictionaries a and b. If a and b have some common keys, b’s values are kept.

core.tools.mergedicts_keep(a, b)¶

return a new dictionary after merging dictionaries a and b. If a and b have some common keys, a’s values are kept.

class core.tools.ErrorCreator(object)¶

This is a small utility to help keep code readable when dealing with errors. Sometimes, when we detect an error, we want to continue and report the error later or report a list of errors instead just one, which saves time when debugging. This is what this class does.

__init__(exception,title = ''):
Parameters:
  • exception – the class of the exception to raise
  • title – a general title to be displayed is the exception is raised
__call__(message)¶

Appends the message to the list of messages to display.

check()¶

If no messages have been added through the __call__ method, nothing happens, otherwise, an exception of the class given in the constructor is raised, initialised with all messages added through the __call__ method separated by a new line.

core.tools.distribute(values)¶

recursively distributes all values contained in values, in other terms, generates all the possible combinations. The parameter values is expected to be a list of any type of element, combinations will be generated if some of these elements are lists:

a = [1,2,3,4]
print distribute(a)
 [[1, 2, 3, 4]]
a = [1,2,3,[4,5]]
print distribute(a)
 [[1, 2, 3, 4], [1, 2, 3, 5]]
a = [1,[2,3],[4,5],[6,7]]
print distribute(a)
 [[1, 2, 4, 6], [1, 2, 4, 7], [1, 2, 5, 6], [1, 2, 5, 7], [1, 3, 4, 6], [1, 3, 4, 7], [1, 3, 5, 6], [1, 3, 5, 7]]
[[1, 2, 3, 4], [1, 2, 3, 5]]
core.tools.nestedLoops(directive, keywords, action = None, **kargs)¶
Parameters:
  • directive (dict) – A dictionary containing keywords. Some keywords are lists, this function executes nested loops given by the keyword keywords and generates as many dictionaries as combinations
  • keywords (list) – list of keywords, the number of items in the list determines the number of nested loops
  • action (callable) – a function to be called for each dictionary generated.

This function executes nested loops given by the keyword keywords and generates as many dictionaries as combinations. If an action is passed, it is called for each combination. The function also returns the list all of combinations. Extra parameters can be passed to the action through **kargs:

d = dictionary(
    direction = ['north','south','west','east'],
    speed = [50,100,150],
    vehicule = ['boat','car'],
    expver = 0001,
)
print nestedLoops(d,['direction','speed','vehicule'])

[{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}, {'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}]

def visitor(directive,current):
    print current
print nestedLoops(d,['direction','speed','vehicule'],visitor)

{'direction': 'north', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'north', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'north', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'north', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'north', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'north', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'south', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'south', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'south', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'south', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'south', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'south', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'west', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'west', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'west', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'west', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'west', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'west', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}
{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'boat'}
{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': 'car'}

nestedLoops(d,['direction','speed'],visitor)

{'direction': 'north', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'north', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'north', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'south', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'south', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'south', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'west', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'west', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'west', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 50, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 100, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
{'direction': 'east', 'speed': 150, 'expver': 1, 'vehicule': ['boat', 'car']}
Note :The list returned by the function is reversed (recursive processing), you can reverse it again if necessary: mylist.reverse()
core.tools.dirlist(dir, follow_symlinks = True)¶
Parameters:
  • dir (str) – A valid os path
  • follow_symlinks (bool) – follow symbolic links

Walks a directory tree and returns a list of the full path to the directories found under dir + dir itself. If required it follows symbolic links.

core.tools.dirwalk(dir, follow_symlinks = True)¶
Parameters:
  • dir (str) – A valid os path
  • follow_symlinks (bool) – follow symbolic links

Walks a directory tree and returns a full path to each of the directories including dir itself. It is implemented as a generator.
